text: A colleague calls you from the operating room stating that they are concerned regarding a bowel duct injury during a lap coli. The gallbladder was long and tubular and there was immediate concern regarding a duct transection. This patient will require a Roux-en-Y hepatic ojejunostomy and you'll see why in a moment. It would seem that there is a tubular structure extending towards the hilar plate consistent with the common hepatic duct. A clip can be seen at the confluence of the cystic duct and the common hepatic duct. There seem to be more clips than would usually be used during a laparoscopic cholecystectomy, and the distal end of the bile duct is seen clipped here. That clip corresponds to the cystic artery, it would seem. The other clips are likely on lymphatics.

The surgeon removes a clip from the common hepatic duct, revealing the bile duct epithelium. Forceps pinch the duct as the cholecystectomy is completed. An endo loop is placed on the gallbladder, away from the bile duct incision. A pediatric feeding tube is introduced through a separate stab incision for cholangiogram preparation. The catheter is inserted into the bile duct, with visible pulses in the right and left hepatic arteries indicating no vascular biliary injury. The catheter is secured with a clip while the duct is flushed to ensure patency for the cholangiogram.

This demonstrates the positioning that was used in this case. I would generally stay away from leaving the left arm out and ensure the bed is positioned in a way that allows for easy introduction of a C-arm for a cholangiogram, as you never know when you might need to do an intraoperative cholangiogram during a lap coli. This demonstrates that the common hepatic duct has been transected. The patient also has an accessory right posterior duct.
The surgeon assesses the lumen and removes the cholecystectomy specimen. Using scissors, the bile duct is sharply opened for repair, revealing healthy bleeding edges from the bile duct and lumen.

The surgeon identifies the ligament of Treitz and runs the small bowel to find a suitable point for transection. Using a stapler, the bowel is divided. A window is created anterior to the duodenum for a retrocolic Roux limb, exposing the pancreatic head and anterior surface of the duodenum. The selected bowel limb is introduced through this window and positioned in the right upper quadrant for Roux-en-Y hepaticojejunostomy formation.

The surgeon positions the camera through a twelve millimeter port in the right flank, providing a view of the duodenum posterior to the loop of jejunum. A corner stitch is placed to bring the bowel up to the common hepatic duct, orienting the structures for an enterogamy. Using Maryland forceps, a ductomucosa anastomosis is initiated posteriorly with interrupted Vivo braided absorbable sutures. The front wall is closed with running five-o braided absorbable sutures, ensuring the lumen remains unobstructed. The anastomosis is tested with water, showing no bile leak, confirmed by the absence of bile on a clean white Raytech gauze.

The surgeon performs a side-to-side enteroenterostomy below the mesentery, orienting the loops of bowel approximately forty centimeters from the hepatico-jejunostomy. Two enterotomies are made, and a stapler is used to create a common channel. The anastomosis is closed with a running suture and re-inspected using an underwater test, confirming no bile leakage.
Drains were left which were removed uneventfully. The patient did very well. He has been seen in follow-up and is doing very well and has returned back to work as a construction worker.
